1: shadow God shall arise, his enemies shall be scattered; and those who hate him shall flee before him! The resurrection is God arising: God raised him up, loosing the pangs of death, because it was not possible for him to be held by it (Acts 2:24). Death and its author scatter before the risen Christ — he disarmed the rulers and authorities (Colossians 2:15), routing them as the enemies flee at God's arising.
2: shadow As smoke is driven away, so you shall drive them away; as wax melts before fire, so the wicked shall perish before God! The wicked melting before God — eschatological imagery: every valley shall be filled, and every mountain and hill shall be made low (Luke 3:5); the elements will melt with fervent heat (2 Peter 3:10). The wicked who opposed Christ are driven away at his vindication.
3: shadow But the righteous shall be glad; they shall exult before God; they shall be jubilant with joy! The joy of the resurrection community: though you have not seen him, you love him. Though you do not now see him, you believe in him and rejoice with joy that is inexpressible and filled with glory (1 Peter 1:8). The righteous exult before God through Christ who is their righteousness (Philippians 3:9).
4: shadow Sing to God, sing praises to his name; lift up a song to him who rides through the deserts; his name is the LORD; exult before him! The one who rides through the deserts is the rider on the white horse (Revelation 19:11-16) — Christ whose name is the Word of God (Revelation 19:13). The LORD whose name is lifted up is the Lord Jesus, at whose name every knee bows (Philippians 2:9-10).
5: revelation of God Father of the fatherless and protector of widows is God in his holy habitation. God's fatherly care for the vulnerable is most fully revealed in Christ, who ministered to the widow of Nain (Luke 7:11-15), honored the widow's offering (Luke 21:1-4), and entrusted his mother to the beloved disciple from the cross (John 19:26-27). James grounds care for orphans and widows in God's holy character (James 1:27).
6: shadow God settles the solitary in a home; he leads out the prisoners to prosperity, but the rebellious dwell in a parched land. Christ's Nazareth sermon cites Isaiah's prisoner-release as his own mission: he has sent me to proclaim liberty to the captives and recovering of sight to the blind, to set at liberty those who are oppressed (Luke 4:18, citing Isaiah 61:1). The Ascension text in Ephesians 4:8 connects to v18 of this psalm — the same liberating theme.
7: type O God, when you went out before your people, when you marched through the wilderness, The Exodus march prefigures Christ leading his people through the wilderness of this age. Paul identifies the wilderness generation's experience with Christ: they drank from the spiritual Rock that followed them, and the Rock was Christ (1 Corinthians 10:4). The divine march through the wilderness is Christ's own pattern of faithfulness through suffering.
8: type the earth quaked, the heavens poured down rain, before God, the One of Sinai, before God, the God of Israel. Sinai's theophany — quaking, rain, fire — is the pattern the NT applies to the Passion and Parousia: earthquake at the crucifixion (Matthew 27:51), fire at Pentecost (Acts 2:3), cosmic signs at the consummation (Revelation 6:12-17). The God of Sinai is the God whose glory shines in the face of Christ (2 Corinthians 4:6).
9: type Rain in abundance, O God, you shed abroad; you restored your inheritance as it languished; The abundant rain as divine provision — the Spirit poured out at Pentecost like rain: in the last days... I will pour out my Spirit on all flesh (Acts 2:17, citing Joel 2:28). Christ restores the languishing inheritance by sending the Spirit of adoption (Galatians 4:6) to renew God's people.
10: shadow your flock found a dwelling in it; in your goodness, O God, you provided for the needy. Christ the Good Shepherd provides for the needy flock: I am the good shepherd... I lay down my life for the sheep (John 10:11). The dwelling the flock finds is the Father's house: in my Father's house are many rooms... I go to prepare a place for you (John 14:2).
11: shadow The Lord gives the word; the women who announce the news are a great host: The first proclaimers of the resurrection were women — a great host carrying the news: Go quickly and tell his disciples that he has risen from the dead (Matthew 28:7). The Lord gives the word of resurrection and chooses women as the first heralds — the great host of those who announce the good news.
12: shadow The kings of the armies — they flee, they flee! — the women at home divide the spoil. The kings who fled at Christ's resurrection — death, sin, the powers — are routed. The spoil divided is the grace poured out: to each one of us grace was given according to the measure of Christ's gift (Ephesians 4:7), which Paul grounds in v18 of this psalm about the Ascension and its gifts.
13: shadow though you men lie among the sheepfolds — the wings of a dove covered with silver, its pinions with shimmering gold. The dove of peace and beauty — the Spirit descending as a dove at Christ's baptism (Matthew 3:16). The shimmering silver and gold of the dove's wings reflect the transfiguration glory: his clothes became radiant, intensely white (Mark 9:3).
14: shadow When the Almighty scatters kings there, let snow fall on Zalmon. The scattering of kings at the Ascension and parousia: all authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me (Matthew 28:18). The transformation of dark mountains to gleaming snow mirrors the resurrection transformation — what was dead and dark becomes luminous under the power of God.
15: shadow O mountain of God, mountain of Bashan; O many-peaked mountain, mountain of Bashan! The many-peaked mountain — a type of Zion, the mountain of God's presence. Christ as the new Zion: you have come to Mount Zion and to the city of the living God, the heavenly Jerusalem (Hebrews 12:22). The mountain where God dwells is now the risen Christ — the meeting place of heaven and earth.
16: shadow Why do you look with hatred, O many-peaked mountain, at the mount that God desired for his abode, yes, where the LORD will dwell forever? God's chosen dwelling is not the impressive peaks but the humble mount — Zion, and ultimately Christ himself: the one whom God has chosen and precious... a cornerstone chosen and precious (1 Peter 2:4-6). The unexpected, humble mountain is where God forever dwells.
17: shadow The chariots of God are twice ten thousand, thousands upon thousands; the Lord is among them; Sinai is now in the sanctuary. The Lord among his innumerable heavenly armies — Christ who said he could call twelve legions of angels (Matthew 26:53) but chose not to. At the Ascension, Christ enters the heavenly sanctuary (Hebrews 9:12) where ten thousand times ten thousand angels surround the throne (Revelation 5:11).
18: direct You ascended on high, leading a host of captives in your train and receiving gifts among men, even among the rebellious, that the LORD God may dwell there. Ephesians 4:8 quotes and transforms this verse to describe Christ's Ascension: When he ascended on high he led a host of captives and gave gifts to men. Paul reads the Ascension as the fulfillment of this victory march — Christ ascending to the Father's right hand and distributing the Spirit's gifts to the church.
19: shadow Blessed be the Lord, who daily bears us up; God is our salvation. Christ daily bearing his people: his continual intercession at the Father's right hand (Romans 8:34; Hebrews 7:25 — he always lives to make intercession for them). God is our salvation — Acts 4:12: there is no other name under heaven given among men by which we must be saved.
20: shadow Our God is a God of salvation, and to GOD, the Lord, belong deliverances from death. Deliverances from death belong supremely to the resurrection: God raised him from the dead, freeing him from the agony of death, because it was impossible for death to keep its hold on him (Acts 2:24). The plural deliverances encompasses not only Christ's resurrection but the resurrection of all who are in him.
21: shadow But God will strike the heads of his enemies, the hairy crown of him who walks in his guilty ways. The ultimate defeat of Satan — the serpent whose head is struck: the God of peace will soon crush Satan under your feet (Romans 16:20). Christ crushes the head of the enemy (Genesis 3:15) through the cross, where the one who walks in guilty ways against the righteous one is condemned (Colossians 2:15).
22: shadow The Lord said, I will bring them back from Bashan, I will bring them back from the depths of the sea, Bringing back from the depths — the resurrection. Christ descends into the depths of death (Ephesians 4:9 — the lower regions of the earth) and is brought back: who will descend into the abyss? (that is, to bring Christ up from the dead) (Romans 10:7). From Bashan to the sea's depths, no depth is beyond the reach of resurrection.
23: shadow that you may strike your feet in their blood, that the tongues of your dogs may have their portion from the foe. The judgment imagery of the victorious return — the eschatological battle of Revelation (Revelation 19:17-21). The graphic language of victory over those who opposed the righteous one is the language of divine vindication at the last day, when the one who was rejected returns as conqueror.
24: shadow Your procession is seen, O God, the procession of my God, my King, into the sanctuary — The triumphant procession into the sanctuary — Christ's entry into the heavenly sanctuary with his own blood: he entered once for all into the holy places, not by means of the blood of goats and calves but by means of his own blood, thus securing an eternal redemption (Hebrews 9:12). The processional into God's presence is the Ascension.
25: shadow the singers in front, the musicians last, between them virgins playing tambourines: The worshiping procession that accompanies Christ into the sanctuary is the church — the great assembly gathered to praise. Hebrews 2:12 (citing Psalms 22:22) shows Christ leading the praise in the midst of the congregation: he is both the processional leader and the chief singer of God's praises.
26: theme Bless God in the great congregation, the LORD, O you who are of Israel's fountain! The great congregation blessing God — the universal church gathered in Christ. Paul's vision: that together you may with one voice glorify the God and Father of our Lord Jesus Christ (Romans 15:6). Israel's fountain is Christ himself — from his fullness we have all received, grace upon grace (John 1:16).
27: shadow There is Benjamin, the least of them, in the lead, the princes of Judah in their throng, the princes of Zebulun, the princes of Naphtali. The unity of the tribes under God's king — fulfilled in Christ who gathers the scattered children of God into one (John 11:52). The twelve tribes assembled become the twelve apostles and the new Israel, the church drawn from every tribe (Revelation 7:4-8).
28: shadow Summon your power, O God, the power by which you have worked for us. The power of God summoned — the resurrection power: what is the immeasurable greatness of his power toward us who believe, according to the working of his great might that he worked in Christ when he raised him from the dead (Ephesians 1:19-20). The power that raised Christ is the power working for his people.
29: shadow Because of your temple at Jerusalem kings shall bear gifts to you. The temple that draws kings to bring gifts is Christ himself — I tell you, something greater than the temple is here (Matthew 12:6). The Magi bring gifts to the infant king (Matthew 2:11); the kings of the earth bring their glory into the new Jerusalem (Revelation 21:24). The universal tribute to Christ fulfills this verse.
30: shadow Rebuke the beasts that dwell among the reeds, the herd of bulls with the calves of the peoples. Trample underfoot those who lust after tribute; scatter the peoples who delight in war. Christ rebukes the powers — the beast imagery of Revelation connects here. He tramples those who delight in war through his cross (Colossians 2:15) and at the parousia scatters those who wage war against the Lamb (Revelation 19:19-21).
31: shadow Nobles shall come from Egypt; Cush shall hasten to stretch out her hands to God. The nations streaming to God — Acts 8:26-40 (the Ethiopian eunuch from Cush who receives Philip's testimony and is baptized). Egypt and Cush as the ends of the earth reaching out to God finds its first fulfillment in the earliest Gentile conversions and its final fulfillment in Revelation 7:9 (all nations before the throne).
32: shadow O kingdoms of the earth, sing to God; sing praises to the Lord, The kingdoms singing to Christ — the kingdom of the world has become the kingdom of our Lord and of his Christ, and he shall reign forever and ever (Revelation 11:15). The singing of the kingdoms anticipates the new song of the redeemed from every kingdom, tribe, and tongue (Revelation 5:9).
33: revelation of God to him who rides in the heavens, the ancient heavens; behold, he sends out his voice, his mighty voice. Christ who rides the heavens: behold, he is coming with the clouds, and every eye will see him (Revelation 1:7). His mighty voice — the voice like the roar of many waters (Revelation 1:15) that John hears at Patmos — is the voice of the one who rides through the heavens and speaks creation into being.
34: shadow Ascribe power to God, whose majesty is over Israel, and whose power is in the skies. Ascribing power to God is ascribing power to Christ, in whom the fullness of God dwells bodily (Colossians 2:9). His majesty over Israel and his power in the skies is the ascended Lord's authority: all authority in heaven and on earth has been given to me (Matthew 28:18).
35: revelation of God Awesome is God from his sanctuary; the God of Israel — he is the one who gives power and strength to his people. Blessed be God! The God who gives power from his sanctuary is the Christ who gives the Spirit: you will receive power when the Holy Spirit has come upon you (Acts 1:8). The awesome God from his sanctuary is the ascended Christ at the Father's right hand, blessing his people with every spiritual blessing (Ephesians 1:3).
